title: The part that uses busyness to avoid feeling anything at all
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that runs the calendar

I know this part well. It's the one that wakes up at 5:30 AM with a to-do list already forming. It's the part that books meetings back to back, fills every evening with obligations, and keeps the weekend schedule packed so tight there's no room to breathe. Look, I'm not talking about being productive. I'm talking about a specific kind of busyness that feels like survival.

Here's the thing. This part has a job. A very specific, very important job. It keeps you from feeling anything at all. Because if you slow down, if you stop moving, if you sit in silence for even five minutes, something might surface. And that something is terrifying.

I remember a period in my late twenties when I was working two jobs, volunteering for three committees, and somehow still finding time to reorganize my closet by color and season. People called me driven. They called me ambitious. I called it not wanting to drown in the ocean of grief that was sitting right beneath the surface of my skin.

Bessel van der Kolk talks about this in his work on trauma and the body. He writes that trauma survivors often develop a hyperactive response system. Not just to threats, but to stillness itself. Stillness feels dangerous. Because stillness is where the body remembers. And the body remembers things the mind has worked very hard to forget.

So this part steps in. It's not the enemy. It's not trying to ruin your life. It's trying to save you from the weight of your own history. It's the part that says, "Keep moving and you won't have to feel the shame, the grief, the rage, the loneliness." And for a while, it works. It works really well.

But here's what I've learned. The body keeps score. And eventually, the body demands payment.

The exhaustion that isn't physical

You know that bone-deep tiredness that sleep doesn't fix? The kind where you wake up and already feel like you've run a marathon? That's not from your schedule. That's from the part that's been running the show. Running on adrenaline. Running on cortisol. Running on sheer willpower and avoidance.

I spent years thinking I was just bad at relaxing. I'd try to meditate and feel like I was going to crawl out of my skin. I'd go on vacation and spend the first three days in a state of low-grade panic because there was nothing to do. Nothing to accomplish. Nothing to distract me from the quiet roar inside.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It helped me see that my busyness wasn't just a habit. It was a coping mechanism learned in childhood. A way to earn love, to prove worth, to stay safe in a home where emotional needs weren't met.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The natural rhythm of contraction and expansion in the nervous system. We contract when we feel threat. We expand when we feel safety. But for trauma survivors, that contraction gets stuck. We stay in a state of chronic activation. And busyness is one of the most socially acceptable forms of chronic activation.

No one questions the person who works 80 hours a week. They get praised. They get promoted. They get called a "go-getter." Meanwhile, their nervous system is screaming for rest. But the part that runs the show doesn't know how to stop. It doesn't trust that stopping is safe.

What happens when you stop

I'm not going to tell you that stopping is easy. It's not. The first time I tried to intentionally slow down, I felt like I was going to die. Not metaphorically. I mean my chest tightened, my heart raced, and I had a full-blown panic attack on my living room floor. Stay with me here. This is normal. This is what happens when the part that uses busyness to avoid feeling finally gets confronted.

The part doesn't go quietly. It fights. It throws up every distraction it can find. It reminds you of all the things you "should" be doing. It whispers that you're lazy, that you're wasting time, that you're going to fall apart if you stop moving. And here's the thing. It's not entirely wrong. You might fall apart. But falling apart isn't the same as being broken. Sometimes falling apart is the only way to let the pieces land where they need to be.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation explains how different parts of us take on different roles to survive. The busy part isn't just a bad habit. It's a protector. It's a firefighter. It's the part that runs into the burning building of your psyche and tries to keep the flames from spreading.

But here's the problem. Firefighters also get burned out. They can't keep fighting fires forever without rest. And neither can this part of you.

I started small. I set a timer for five minutes of doing nothing. Just sitting. No phone, no book, no music. Just me and the silence. The first few times, I lasted about thirty seconds before I reached for my phone. But I kept trying. And slowly, something shifted. The panic started to quiet. The restlessness started to settle. And underneath all that noise, I found something I'd been running from for decades.

I found grief. I found rage. I found a loneliness so vast it felt like an ocean. And I found that I could survive it. I could sit with it. I could let it move through me without being destroyed.

The part that needs a new job

This part doesn't need to be eliminated. It needs to be retired. Given a new assignment. Because the energy it carries isn't bad. The drive, the discipline, the ability to get things done. Those are gifts. But they need to be used in service of life, not in service of avoidance.

I've learned to talk to this part. To thank it for keeping me safe all those years. To acknowledge the sacrifice it made. And then to gently suggest that maybe, just maybe, it can take a break now. That I'm not a child anymore. That I have resources. That I have a nervous system that can learn to regulate.

Dan Siegel talks about the window of tolerance. The zone where we can feel our feelings without being overwhelmed by them. When we're constantly busy, we're living outside that window. We're either hyperaroused (anxious, frantic, driven) or hypoaroused (numb, checked out, exhausted). The goal isn't to stay busy forever. The goal is to widen the window. To build capacity for stillness. To learn that feeling doesn't have to kill us.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on accepting ourselves exactly as we are, including our protective parts, has been deeply helpful for me. Because this part needs acceptance, not rejection. It needs to be seen, not shamed. It needs to know that it's allowed to rest.

I'm not going to tell you that you can quit your job and spend a year in a monastery. That's not realistic for most of us. But you can start with five minutes. You can start by noticing the impulse to fill every empty space with activity. You can start by asking the part, "What are you afraid would happen if we stopped?"

And then you can listen. Really listen. Without judgment. Without trying to fix. Just listen.

Because underneath that fear, there's usually a younger part. A part that learned that stillness meant danger. That quiet meant abandonment. That being busy was the only way to be loved. And that part needs something different now. It needs to know that you're here. That you're grown. That you can handle whatever comes up.

The practice of being with what is

I still struggle with this. I won't pretend I've mastered it. Some days I still fill my calendar to the brim and pretend I don't know why. But I catch myself faster now. I notice the tightness in my chest. The way my thoughts race ahead to the next task. The familiar hum of avoidance dressed up as productivity.

And when I notice, I stop. I take a breath. I put my hand on my heart and say, "I see you. I know you're trying to protect me. And we're okay right now. We can slow down."

Sometimes it works. Sometimes it doesn't. And that's okay. This isn't about perfection. It's about practice. It's about building a new relationship with the part that uses busyness to avoid feeling. A relationship based on compassion instead of control.

Wild, right? That the thing you've been fighting for so long might actually just need to be heard. That the part you've been trying to silence might be the very part that needs your voice the most.

Sit with that for a moment. Notice what just happened in your body. Did your shoulders drop? Did your breath deepen? Did you feel a flicker of something soft and sad? That's the part. It's right there. It's been there all along. Waiting for you to notice. Waiting for you to stop running long enough to say, "I see you. I hear you. And I'm not leaving."

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect specifically
